sources.summary: The paper uses the SMC star-formation history as evidence for tidal triggering, noting bursts tied to Milky Way pericenters and a 3-4 Gyr feature requiring external gas infall.

quotes.quote: In the Small Magellanic Cloud, the number of stars at a given age has been determined by Zaritsky Harris (2004). They noticed that the derived SMC star formation history reveals some bursts corresponding to pericenters of its orbit around the Milky Way. A model taking the tidal triggering into account fits better the observations, although the quantitative influence of the tide is uncertain, between 10 and 70 of the star formation could be tidal in origin, cf figure zar-f11 .

One of the problem in the fit comes from the too large increase in star formation about 3-4 Gyrs ago, which can only be explained by external gas accretion. A significant gas infall, by about 50 of the total gas mass, improves the fit, and accounts also for the observed age-metallicity relation.

sources.summary: The paper uses SMC cluster ages and metallicities to infer its chemical enrichment, inner/outer disk age differences, a 2.5 Gyr star-formation burst, and a radial metallicity gradient.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: SMC optical centre (cross), assumed to be placed (J2000) at: 00 ^h 52 ^m 45 ^s , -72 49 43

quotes.quote: By combining the present cluster sample with 19 additional SMC clusters with ages and metallicities in a homogeneous scale, we analise the age and metallicity distributions in different regions of the SMC to probe the galaxy chemical enrichment and its spatial distribution. Very few clusters younger than 4 Gyr are found in the outer disk and, conversely, very few clusters older than 4 Gyr lie in the inner disk. Furthermore, the present age-position relation for the SMC clusters in the inner disk suggests not only the possibility that the clusters were formed outside in, like in a relatively rapid collapse, but also that the inner disk itself could have been formed during a bursting formation mechanism, with an important cluster formation event centred at 2.5 Gyr. According to the recent results obtained by , this cluster burst, which occurred 2.5 Gyr ago, is clearly related to an episode of enhanced star formation having taken place about the same time ago. Evidence is also presented on the existence of a radial metal abundance gradient for the SMC disk, which reflects the combination between an older and more metal-poor population of clusters distributed throughout the SMC and a younger and metal-richer one mainly formed in the inner disk.

sources.summary: The paper uses the SMC alongside the LMC to examine Cepheid metallicity effects and reports eclipsing-binary work deriving a distance of 60 kpc to the SMC.

quotes.quote: Several studies have taken advantage of the difference in mean chemical abundance between the Small and Large Magellanic Clouds ( 0.5 dex) to characterize the effect using the Cepheids located in these galaxies. sasselov97 used EROS photometry of SMC and LMC Cepheids to characterize the metallicity effect in the B band at 0.4 mag/dex. udalski01 relied on OGLE photometry of LMC, SMC and IC1613 Cepheids and other distance indicators (red clump stars, RR Lyrae variables, and the tip of the red giant branch) and found no discernible differential effects in the zeropoints of these methods.

harries03 recently presented first results from an ongoing program to study over 100 eclipsing binaries in the SMC, using OGLE photometry and radial velocities obtained in multiplexed fashion with the 2dF spectrograph. They derived a distance of 60 4 kpc to the SMC, primarily based on 3 DEBs and 6 semi-detached systems.

sources.summary: The paper models the SMC as part of the Magellanic System, emphasizing its interaction with the LMC and Milky Way, shorter perigalactic cycle, and present gravitational binding to the LMC.

quotes.quote: The basis are the new detailed H I observations of the Magellanic System (including the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC) and the Small Magellanic Cloud (SMC)) by Br uns et al. ( ). As remnants of the LMC--SMC--MW interaction, extended structures connected to the System are observed.

It is also notable that in the models with aspherical halos, the SMC period of perigalactic approaches is significantly shorter than the period of the LMC and that the SMC remains closer than 100 kpc to the MW center during the last 4 Gyr. When the MW DM flattening q 1.0 , the LMC and SMC orbital cycle lengths were comparable for the model B. Independently of the MW halo shape, the LMC and SMC are currently forming a gravitationally bound couple in our models.

sources.summary: The paper treats SMC microlensing as tied to a tidally stretched bar, non-equilibrium line-of-sight structure, significant self-lensing, and interaction models with SMC deformation.

quotes.quote: The related issue on the efficiency of self-lensing of a tidally stretched SMC bar has also been brought up a few times (Alcock et al. 1997c, Palanque-Delabrouille et al. 1998, Zhao 1998a), but was highlighted soon after the discovery of the 98-SMC-1 caustic binary event (Afonso et al. 1998, Alcock et al. 1999, Albrow et al. 1999, Becker et al. 1998, Sahu Sahu 1998, Udalski et al. 1998). The observations are clearly in support of the idea that the SMC has a non-equilibrium structure extended in the line of sight (Caldwell Coulson 1986, Mathewson, Ford Visvanathan 1986, Welch et al. 1987, Westerlund 1997). While there is still some dispute about the interpretation of the two binary lens candidates LMC-9 (Bennett et al. 1996, Zhao 1998a) and 98-SMC-1 (Honma 1999, Kerins Evans 1999), the consensus seems to be that self-lensing in the LMC and SMC is significant if not dominant.

These include a completely unbound grand tidal arm of the MCs which extends to Ursa Minor and Draco, a localized common halo bridging the MCs, a tidally strongly deformed SMC, a polar ring structure or a tidal halo of the LMC (cf. Fig. 2).

All these models imply tidal interaction and could match observations, such as, the Magellanic Stream and its leading arm, proper motions of Ursa Minor and Draco, distributions of gas and stellar associations in the Magellanic Bridge, and a possible large line of sight extent of the SMC and a polar ring structure seen in the velocity distribution of LMC carbon stars (Kunkel et al. 1997).

sources.summary: The paper includes SMC binary-microlensing events in its rotation analysis, concluding that lens-binary rotation can matter for self-lensing in the SMC disk.

quotes.quote: To this time, two binary-lens microlensing events have been observed, MACHO LMC-9 and MACHO 98-SMC-1. Although we cannot say for certain from only these events, these events support self-lensing because of slow transverse velocities.
Since the characteristic transverse velocity of a lens in the LMC disk or the SMC disk is smaller than that in our halo, the rotation of the lens binary in the disk may be important.

We investigated the rotation effects of lens binaries on the binary-microlensing events towards the LMC and SMC. It is found that the rotation effects cannot always be neglected when the lens binaries are in the LMC disk or the SMC disk, while the rotation can be neglected when the lenses are in our halo.

sources.summary: The paper studies stellar spatial structure in the SMC, finding initially fractal stellar distributions whose substructure is erased on roughly the galaxy crossing time by galactic dynamics.

quotes.quote: We present an analysis of the spatial distribution of various stellar populations within the Large and Small Magellanic Clouds. We use optically selected stellar samples with mean ages between 9 and 1000 Myr, and existing stellar cluster catalogues to investigate how stellar structures form and evolve within the LMC/SMC. We use two statistical techniques to study the evolution of structure within these galaxies, the Q parameter and the two-point correlation function (TPCF). In both galaxies we find the stars are born with a high degree of substructure (i.e. are highly fractal) and that the stellar distribution approaches that of the 'background' population on timescales similar to the crossing times of the galaxy ( 80 Myr 150 Myr for the SMC/LMC respectively).

sources.summary: The paper contrasts the SMC with the Milky Way, citing its different abundances, photometric star-formation history, early stellar formation, quiescent interval, later bursts, and tidal triggering.

quotes.quote: The SMC and Milky Way have had very different star formation histories, and thus have different elemental abundances. The star formation history of the SMC has recently been investigated by HZ04 based on deep photometric surveys of the entire SMC. They find that most of the stars in the SMC formed 8.5 Gyr ago (corresponding to redshift z 1.2 ) with a period of quiescence lasting 3 Gyr, followed by more recent bursts in star formation. Tidal interactions with the Milky Way likely play a roll in causing star formation bursts in the SMC HZ04 .

sources.summary: The paper links the SMC to the Magellanic Bridge, a recent close encounter with the LMC, tidally triggered star formation, and a Be/X-ray-binary-rich high-energy population.

quotes.quote: Multiwavelength studies of the SMC have shown that it contains a large number of X-ray binary pulsars -- all but one of them in Be/X-ray binary systems CoeCorbetMcGowan2009,CoeEdgeGalache2005 . This preponderance of young systems is most likely explained by tidally triggered star formation precipitated by the most recent close approach of the SMC and LMC GardinerNoguchi1996 . However, the most recent close approach of the SMC and LMC was 200 Myr ago -- much longer than the evolutionary timescale of Be/X-ray binaries, which implies that either there has been a significant delay between the encounter of the SMC and LMC and the onset of star formation, or that subsequent waves of star formation have given rise to these Be/X-ray binaries HarrisZaritsky2004 .
With the mass of the LMC being 10 that of the SMC, tidal interactions between the galaxies would have a much greater effect on the SMC, and this may be reflected by the differing stellar populations.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes SMC infrared region photometry, finding weaker cumulative region fluxes than the LMC while comparing SED shapes and luminosity functions across the Magellanic Clouds.

quotes.quote: With the aperture photometry, we computed IR SEDs using the total cumulative Spitzer IRAC (3.6, 4.5, 8 m) and MIPS (24, 70, 160 m) fluxes of the LMC, SMC, and each region. The cumulative SED of the LMC is brighter than the SMC, and their shapes are consistent with IR SEDs of the Magellanic Clouds in the literature. The SMC regions have cumulative fluxes that are weaker, by about an order of magnitude, than the LMC regions. We argue that this is expected from what is known about the LMC and SMC region luminosity functions.

sources.summary: The paper derives near-infrared period-luminosity relations for SMC Type II Cepheids and uses them to obtain an SMC distance modulus consistent with other distance determinations.

quotes.quote: We presented precision J and K_s band photometry of 19 Type II Cepheids in the SMC. The slope of the resulting J and K_s band P-L relations were found to be, within the errors, in good agreement with the corresponding slopes obtained for Type II Cepheids in the LMC (M09), Galactic bulge (Groenewegen, Udalski and Bono) and globular clusters (M06). Assuming the fiducial P-L relation of M06 the following SMC distance modulus was derived from our data: (m-M) _ 0 = 18.85 0.07 (statistical) 0.07 (systematic) This result agrees very well with recent distance determinations to the SMC based on other techniques.

Moreover, the differencial distances between the LMC, SMC and Galactic Bulge derived from Type II Cepheids are very similar to those obtained from Cepheids, TRGB, RR Lyrae stars and red clump stars, which suggests that the population effects on the slope and/or zero point of the Type II Cepheid P-L relation must be small.

sources.summary: The paper portrays SMC as a nearby gas-rich, actively star-forming dwarf galaxy with one-fifth solar gas metallicity, whose reservoir has been depleted by LMC interactions that formed the star-forming Magellanic Bridge.

quotes.quote: The more massive or more isolated among them have been able to retain enough gas to sustain star formation over prolonged periods of time right till the present day, leading to the buildup of chemical richesse (the LMC's gas has a metal content 1/2 that of the Sun, and the SMC 1/5 ). These gas-rich dwarf galaxies are both tantalising relics of the Universe's re-birth during the epoch of cosmic re-ionization, as well as fascinating miniature galaxies in their own right. The Magellanic Clouds are a splendid showcase of galaxy interaction. The SMC's reservoir of gas has been depleted by the pull from the significantly more massive LMC, forming the Magellanic Bridge that connects the two --- star formation is occurring in the denser portions of this tidal feature.

sources.summary: The paper treats the SMC as a key Magellanic satellite analogue, citing its luminosity, star-formation rate, proximity, likely pairing with the LMC, and rarity in simulations.

quotes.quote: In this paper we focus on satellites that are near analogues of the two most luminous objects around the MW, the Large and Small Magellanic Clouds (LMC and SMC, collectively MC, henceforth) which are characterised by quite high masses and luminosities (much higher at least than any other galaxies within several hundred kpc of the Milky Way), ongoing SF indicating substantial gas reservoirs, and their nearness to us (50 and 60 kpc for the LMC and SMC respectively). They also appear to be close to one another, probably forming a bound pair, with an interaction history that may be of great importance, both for their SF histories and for the origin of the Magellanic Stream of H i gas. For comparison with the satellite galaxies found in the present study, we assume R band luminosities of 1.6 10 ^9 and 3.7 10 ^8 L _ , and SF rates of 0.17 and 0.027 M _ yr ^ 1 for the LMC and SMC respectively.
Similarly, they find the probabilities of having a second satellite as massive as the SMC to be in the range 3.3-20 .

sources.summary: The paper cites SMC H I studies as a test case for turbulence statistics, saying genus curves were measured there and that warm H I is transonic while the cold component is supersonic.

quotes.quote: The use of genus statistics for the study of HI was first discussed in Lazarian (1999), and subsequent studies presented the genus curves for the SMC (Lazarian et al. 2002; Lazarian 2004, Chepurnov et al. 2008) and for MHD simulations (Kowal, Lazarian Beresnyak 2007).

For studies of neutral gas, i.e. 21 cm HI gas, the warm phase of the SMC was shown to have properties of transonic gas (Burkhart et al. 2010). In the case of the cold component, several independent statistical and direct measurements applied to the spectral lines and the column density indicate this component of the SMC is supersonic (Stanimirovic Lazarian 2001, Burkhart et al. 2010).
An example of successful application of such techniques to the Small Magellanic Cloud (SMC) HI data is provided in Burkhart et al. (2010).

sources.summary: The paper compares Phoenix with the SMC, noting similar recent star-formation epochs, low metallicity, much larger SMC mass, and the SMC's unusually rich HMXB population.

quotes.quote: These epochs of star formation are similar to those for another dIrr galaxy: the Small Magellanic Cloud (SMC). Zaritsky03 report that the SMC had ``bursts'' of star formation at 2.5, 0.4 and 0.06 Gyr in the past. In fact, the Phoenix dwarf galaxy shares many other common features with the SMC, including its low metallicity (Z=0.004 for the SMC, Russell92 ; Z=0.0015 for Phoenix, H09). The SMC is 100 times as massive as Phoenix and host to an unusually high number of High-Mass X-ray Binaries (HMXBs) Coe05 .

sources.summary: The paper cites SMC observations where DIBs and the 2175 extinction bump appear in the quiet wing but are absent in active bar sightlines, likely due to shocks or UV radiation.

quotes.quote: Cox et al. \ (2007a) studied the 2175 extinction bump and DIBs of the SMC. They reported the detection of DIBs toward one line of sight in the relatively quiescent SMC wing region where the 2175 extinction feature is also present. In contrast, neither DIBs nor the 2175 bump were detected toward five lines of sight in the SMC bar which pass through active star formation regions. They argued that the carriers of the 2175 extinction bump and DIBs may be subject to the same physical and chemical processes: both carriers in the SMC bar could be destroyed by shocks or the hard ambient UV radiation; or alternatively, the harsh environment may prohibit the formation of both carriers.

sources.summary: The paper treats the SMC as an independent MOND object, arguing it is nearly circular, unbound from the LMC, and a possible source of Magellanic Stream gas.

quotes.quote: The Small Magellanic Cloud (SMC, presently at a distance of 63 kpc), considered as an independent object unperturbed by the LMC, is nearly on a circular orbit in MOND (black dotted line on Fig. 2). Note that the SMC-LMC pair is typically in a region where the external field from the MW dominates its internal dynamics, and the high relative velocity of the SMC w..t. the LMC then implies that the two are not bound in the MOND context. The Magellanic stream would then have to be due to recent ram-pressure stripping of gas from the SMC since the stream contain very few stars. A recent encounter of the LMC and SMC might have released some gas clouds from the gas-rich SMC, and these gas clouds were further stretched by ram-pressure with the denser clouds leading the less dense ones.

sources.summary: The paper adopts precise SMC proper motions, notes internal-kinematic uncertainties, and finds its predicted motion and orbital pole consistent with the common satellite-plane direction.

quotes.quote: For the LMC and Small Magellanic Cloud (SMC) we adopt the most recent PM measurements by 2013ApJ...764..161K , based on a 7 yr baseline covered by three epochs of Hubble Space Telescope ( HST ) data. The resulting PMs are very precise and the uncertainties are dominated by limitations in our understanding of the internal kinematics and geometry of the LMC and SMC, according to 2013ApJ...764..161K .
This is particularly true for the LMC and SMC, which have tightly constrained PM measurements overlapping well with the predicted range.

The orbital poles of at least six satellites (LMC, SMC, Ursa Minor, Leo II, Draco and Fornax) are closely aligned with each other, being inclined by less than 27^ \ from their average direction (which points to [ l,b ] = [ 177^ ,-3^ ] ) \ and close to the minor axis of the VPOS.

sources.summary: The paper argues that finding a bipolar SNR in the low-metallicity SMC is notable given its small SNR population and lower star-formation rate than the Milky Way.

quotes.quote: The identification of a bipolar SNR in the SMC is remarkable given that only 25 SNRs are known in the SMC bad10 . By comparison, only one SNR in the MW has been shown to be bipolar to date lopez13 out of 300 SNRs green09 , an order of magnitude larger sample. Yet if the SN rate is proportional to the star formation rate (SFR) of a galaxy, then the number of bipolar SNe in the MW should be a factor of 20 greater than the SMC (assuming a SFR of 1 M_ year ^ 1 in the MW and of 0.05 M_ year ^ 1 in the SMC; wilke04,rob10 ). Thus, a bipolar SNR in the SMC may suggest that jet-driven explosions may occur more commonly in the SMC, a low metallicity galaxy (with 0.2 Z_ : russell92 ).

sources.summary: The paper treats the SMC as a massive, out-of-equilibrium Milky Way satellite whose HST proper motions enter orbit integrations; it adopts a 3e9 solar-mass value for the SMC.

quotes.quote: HST proper-motion measurements (Kallivayallil et al. 2006, Kallivayallil et al. 2013, henceforth K13) of the Large and Small Magellanic Clouds (LMC, SMC) have been used to derive past orbits of these two satellites (Besla et al. 2007, henceforth B07), and of Leo I (Sohn et al. 2013; Boylan-Kolchin et al. 2013). Masses of dwarf galaxies remain however highly uncertain. This is particularly true of the most massive ones (Sgr, LMC, SMC) that are significantly out of equilibrium (Walker et al. 2009; McConnachie 2012).

Given the measured 3d position and velocity, ( x , v ) , derived from HST proper motions of the the Sgr dwarf (Pryor et al. 2010) and the LMC and SMC (K13), we integrate the equations of motion backwards in time as in prior work (B07).
For the LMC, we employ the mass most directly motivated by imaging surveys (Saha et al. 2010), which gives 3 10^ 10 M_ for the LMC, and we adopt 3 10^ 9 M_ for the SMC.

sources.summary: The paper uses the SMC orbit and binary history with the LMC to argue that high-mass LMC models are needed to keep the Clouds bound for several gigayears.

quotes.quote: Moreover, the mass of the LMC also controls the orbit of the Small Magellanic Cloud, ultimately determining how long the two galaxies have interacted with each other as a binary pair.

Note as well that high-mass LMC models, > 10^ 11 M _ , are necessary in models of the formation of the Magellanic Stream as they allow for a long-lived ( 4 Gyr) LMC/SMC binary configuration. The relative velocity between the Clouds is 130 km/s; high-mass LMC models (masses of order 10^ 11 M _ ) are needed to explain how the LMC can have held on to the SMC if it is moving at such speeds.
Based on their parameter space search, and the requirement that the LMC and SMC have been a long-lived binary, we adopt a canonical mass model for the LMC = 1.8 10^ 11 M _ .

sources.summary: The SMC is described as a gas-rich, star-forming Milky Way satellite whose likely first infall with the LMC and continued star formation limit its quenching time.

quotes.quote: This trend has just a few exceptions: 4 gas-rich, star-forming galaxies persist within the halos of the MW (the LMC and SMC) and M31 (LGS 3 and IC 10), and 4 - 5 quiescent, gas-poor galaxies reside well beyond of either the MW or M31: Cetus Lewis2007 , Tucana Fraternali2009 , KKR 25 Makarov2012 , KKs 3 Karachentsev2015 , and possibly Andromeda XVIII, though Cetus and Tucana may have orbited within the MW halo Teyssier2012 .

By this definition, the only star-forming, gas-rich satellites are: LMC ( =1.5 10^9 , / 0.3 ) and SMC ( =4.6 10^8 , / 1 ) around the MW, LGS 3 ( =9.6 10^5 , / 0.4 ) and IC 10 ( =9 10^7 , / 0.6 ) around M31.

The 3-D orbital velocity measured for the LMC/SMC strongly suggests that they are experiencing first infall and crossed inside of the MW 2 ago Kallivayalil2013 . Given that the LMC and SMC remain star-forming, this places a lower limit to their quenching timescale (gray triangle), consistent with our statistical timescales.

sources.summary: The paper treats the Small Magellanic Cloud as an exceptional nearby-galaxy laboratory hosting an unusually large, homogeneous population of high-mass X-ray binaries, mainly Be X-ray binaries.

quotes.quote: The Small Magellanic Cloud (SMC) is peculiar because it hosts exceptionally many known HMXBs. So far, the optical counterpart is only in one (well-confirmed) case a super-giant star (SXP 5.05 = SMC X-3; e.g. 1978A&A....66..283C,1980ApJ...237..125C ), while in all other identified cases a Be star (with Balmer emission lines) was found, forming a Be/X-ray binary (BeXRB). The last comprehensive catalogue of HMXBs in the SMC was published by 2005A&A...442.1135L .
The large population of HMXBs in the SMC, in particular Be X-ray binaries, provides the largest homogeneous sample of such systems for statistical population studies.

sources.summary: The paper maps SMC star-forming regions and argues that in its low-metallicity environment, CII traces extended CO-dark molecular hydrogen while CO traces only dense gas.

quotes.quote: This paper presents , and CO emission line maps of the star-forming regions N 66, N 25+N 26, and N 88 in the metal-poor Local Group dwarf galaxy SMC.

A radiative transfer analysis shows that the line is the dominant tracer of (CO-dark) molecular hydrogen in the SMC. CO emission traces only a minor fraction of the total amount of gas. The similarity of the spatial distribution and line profile shape, and the dominance of molecular gas associated with rather than CO emission imply that in the low-metallicity environment of the SMC the small amount of dense molecular gas traced by CO is embedded in the much more extended molecular gas traced only by emission.

sources.summary: The paper treats the SMC as a likely source of Leading Arm gas, linking SMC-like metallicity, possible stripped molecular material, and lower-metallicity model fits to SMC stripping.

quotes.quote: Theoretical models and observational evidence show that both the MS and LA were formed via ram-pressure stripping and tidal interaction between the LMC and SMC (see e.g. DB11,For13 ).

They find that the metallicity of this HVC is similar to the SMC, and the detection of H _ 2 suggests that either the H _ 2 formed in situ or within the SMC and survived tidal stripping. They prefer the latter scenario given that the formation timescale of H _ 2 is long ( 10^ 8 yr). If the LA is stripped from the SMC as suggested by simulations (e.g., DB11 ), we would expect the LA region has a similar H _ 2 and metallicity content as the SMC, and star formation potentially could occur.
A lower metallicity model is a better fit for the majority of the data points, which suggests that the gas was likely stripped from the SMC.

sources.summary: The paper identifies SMC as an individually detected nearby ray source, removes it from the point-source SFG stack because it is extended, and notes its FIR luminosity is atypically low for the sample.

quotes.quote: While the majority of galaxies in our universe produce a ray flux far below the sensitivity of current instruments, a handful of galaxies have been individually detected. These include several of our closest neighbors (LMC, SMC and M31), as well as some of the most intense nearby star-forming galaxies (NGC 1068, NGC 3034 (M 82), NGC 4945, and Arp 220) Acero:2015hja, Peng:2016nsx .

Second, we have removed the LMC, SMC and M31 from this sample, as they are extended ray sources that may not be accurately modeled with the point-source analysis technique employed here. Additionally, the FIR luminosities for these systems are significantly smaller than the typical galaxies in our analysis. Detailed ray analyses of these galaxies exist in the literature 2010A&A...523L...2A, 2015PhRvD..91j2001B, 2015arXiv150103460P, 2016PhRvD..93f2004C , and we will compare our results to these systems in the discussion.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes four Small Magellanic Cloud H II regions with deep VLT spectra, finding lower oxygen abundance than the LMC, similar S/Cl/Ne ratios, and nitrogen differences linked to each galaxy's chemical evolution.

quotes.quote: We obtained echelle spectra of 8 H ii regions in the MCs, 4 in the Small Magellanic Cloud (SMC) and 4 in the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC).

We can see that the O abundance is higher for the LMC than the SMC by 0.38 dex. In Figure abund_fig3 we plot the total abundance for the other elements with respect to oxygen as a function of the ionization degree. The S and Cl abundances are similar in both clouds within the uncertainties; the same behavior is seen for the Ne abundances. For N abundances it seems to be a separation between the two MCs due to differences in the chemical evolution of each galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper treats the Small Magellanic Cloud as the surveyed system for BeXRB counterparts, mapping its Bar and Wing, finding 9808 H-emitting sources, and deriving a 13.3 OBe/OB fraction across the galaxy.

quotes.quote: The Small Magellanic Cloud (SMC) hosts a large number of high-mass X-ray binaries, and in particular of Be/X-ray Binaries (BeXRBs; neutron stars orbiting OBe-type stars), offering a unique laboratory to address the effect of metalicity.

We performed a survey of the SMC Bar and Wing regions using wide-field cameras (WFI@MPG/ESO and MOSAIC@CTIO/Blanco) in order to identify the counterparts of the sources detected in our XMM-Newton survey of the same area.

Our survey reveals 9808 H emitting sources in the SMC. This is 2 to 4 times more sources from other previous surveys (1844 sources; ), mostly due to our deeper coverage down to V =18.5 mag instead of R 16.5 mag. From our analysis we know the number of OB stars and the corresponding number of emission-line stars (i.e. OBe). This allows us to derive the OBe/OB fraction for each field. We find an average value of 13.3 across the SMC, consistent with previous studies (e.g. 5-11 from ).

sources.summary: The SMC entry includes its single old globular cluster and notes that additional Milky Way clusters may once have belonged to the Magellanic Clouds.

quotes.quote: The SMC and LMC Following 2010MNRAS.406.1967G , we include the single old GC (NGC 121) in the SMC, and the 16 old GCs in the LMC, to give the total mass of the GC system in each galaxy. We note that a number of Milky Way GCs have been suggested to once belong to the Magellanic Clouds, e.g. Rup 106 to the SMC 1995AJ....110.1664F . As such, our estimates of the GC system mass in the Clouds are likely to be lower bounds.

sources.summary: The review describes the SMC B[e] supergiant census: six new reported objects, five from major spectroscopic or photometric surveys, with only one confirmed and one strong candidate after inspection.

quotes.quote: Small Magellanic Cloud In the SMC, a total of six new B [ e ] SGs has been reported. Five of them were discovered as by-products from the spectroscopic survey of the hot, luminous stars in the SMC (2dFS, ), from the Runaways and Isolated O Star Spectroscopic Survey of the SMC (RIOTS4, ), and from the SMC photometric catalog ( ).

From a critical inspection of the properties of these six stars, only one object appears to fulfill all required criteria, one object appears as very promising candidate that lacks only some complementary information for unambiguous classification, whereas the remaining four stars are considered as misclassified.
Moving out to the MCs, the numbers amount to 13 (+2) in the LMC and 5 (+1) in the SMC.

sources.summary: The paper finds the SMC has highly uniform H I turbulent properties, with no local feedback enhancement, possibly due to large-scale driving or line-of-sight depth.

quotes.quote: The new methods were applied on the HI observations of the Small and Large Magellanic Clouds (SMC and LMC). In the case of the SMC, we find highly uniform turbulent properties of HI, with no evidence for local enhancements of turbulence due to stellar feedback. Such properties could be caused by a significant turbulent driving on large-scales. Alternatively, a significant line-of-sight depth of the SMC could be masking out localized regions with a steeper SPS slope caused by stellar feedback.

sources.summary: The paper treats the SMC as a nearby dwarf-galaxy signal source whose black holes would produce a sky over-density at its bulk velocity, but whose peak strains are weaker and excluded from simulations.

quotes.quote: Closer to home, the Large (LMC) and Small Magellanic Clouds (SMC) are potential sources of signals. The contribution from either of these two dwarf galaxies would be an additional over-density of signals in a particular part of the sky and within a small range of Doppler shifts, since the black holes are traveling at the respective galaxy's bulk velocity (68 km/s for the LMC and 5 km/s for the SMC ). The LMC and SMC are an order of magnitude further than the Galactic Center , so the peak strains are an order of magnitude below the peak of the Milky Way distribution. Since the expected signal is small and the star formation histories of the LMC and SMC are very uncertain, we do not include them in our simulations.

sources.summary: The paper maps intrinsic reddening across 34 deg^2 of the SMC using background-galaxy SEDs, finding enhanced reddening in the main body and agreement with young-star maps after depth corrections.

quotes.quote: We present a map of the total intrinsic reddening across 34 deg ^ 2 of the Small Magellanic Cloud (SMC) derived using optical ( ugriz ) and near-infrared (IR; YJK_ s ) spectral energy distributions (SEDs) of background galaxies. The reddening map is created using a subsample of 29,274 galaxies with low levels of intrinsic reddening based on the lephare ^ 2 minimisation SED-fitting routine. We find statistically significant enhanced levels of reddening associated with the main body of the SMC compared with regions in the outskirts [ E(B-V) 0.3 mag ] . A comparison with literature reddening maps of the SMC shows that, after correcting for differences in the volume of the SMC sampled, there is good agreement between our results and maps created using young stars.

sources.summary: The paper maps an eastern foreground red-clump structure around the SMC, finding it spatially limited, kinematically distinct from the main body, and likely tidally stripped from the SMC inner disk.

quotes.quote: To determine the spatial extent and properties of this foreground population, we analysed data from the Gaia data release 2 (DR2) of a 314 deg ^2 region centred on the SMC, which cover the entire SMC and a significant portion of the MB. We find that the foreground population is present only between 2 . ^ 5 to 5 ^ -6 ^ from the centre of the SMC in the eastern regions, towards the MB and hence does not fully overlap with the MB in the plane of the sky. The foreground stellar population is found to be kinematically distinct from the stellar population of the main body with 35 km s ^ 1 slower tangential velocity and moving to the North-West relative to the main body. Though the observed properties are not fully consistent with the simulations, a comparison indicates that the foreground stellar structure is most likely a tidally stripped counterpart of the gaseous MB and might have formed from the inner disc (dominated by stars) of the SMC.

sources.summary: The paper presents the Small Magellanic Cloud as a nearby, resolvable galaxy rich in Cepheids and important for testing metallicity effects on the Leavitt Law.

quotes.quote: Of particular importance are the Large and Small Magellanic Clouds (LMC and SMC, respectively). These galaxies are relatively nearby, making them excellent targets for stellar population studies as their individual stars can be resolved. Notably, they each have a substantial Cepheid population, with the Optical Gravitational Lensing Experiment (OGLE) Udalski1999 cataloguing 5000 fundamental-mode Classical Cepheids across both galaxies.

Although the SMC is not used in anchoring the distance scale, it serves as a key galaxy for examining the effect of metallicity on the LL, as it is more metal-poor than both the LMC and Milky Way (MW).

sources.summary: The paper separates NGC 419 from Small Magellanic Cloud field stars, measures the SMC proper motion, and interprets a nearby young component as Magellanic Bridge stars.

quotes.quote: In fact, the proper motions we measured allow us to separate cluster stars, which move on average with ( _ ^ NGC 419 , _ ^ NGC 419 ) = ( +0.878  0.055 ,  1.246  0.048 ) mas yr ^ 1 , from those of the Small Magellanic Cloud and those belonging to a third kinematic feature that we recognise as part of the Magellanic Bridge.

We therefore recognise the main feature as the one describing the stellar component of the main body of the SMC. Its 3 clipped mean absolute proper motion amounts to _ ^ SMC = +0.777  0.005  0.055 mas yr ^ 1 _ ^ SMC = -1.141  0.003  0.048 mas yr ^ 1 , where the second term of the associated uncertainty represents the error on the absolute proper motion zero points. Our field lies about 1 degree off of the nominal centre of the SMC, which is moving away from us with a radial velocity of 145.6 km s ^ 1 ( harris06 ), at a distance of 63 kpc ( cioni2000 ).

sources.summary: The paper discusses the SMC as a star-forming galaxy gamma-ray source, noting its cutoff power-law spectrum, log-parabola modeling choice, and a low-energy SED bin not well explained.

quotes.quote: For a well-known SFG, the Small Magellanic Cloud (SMC), its spectrum with an exponentially cutoff power-law form implied that there was a transition from advection to diffusion in CR propagation Lopez2018 . The spectrum of the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC) was similar to that of the SMC in the GeV energy band e.g., Tang2017 . This similar spectral feature implies that there may be a similar particle evolution process inside both SFGs. However, there is currently no convincing evidence to confirm this conjecture. The uncertainty of particle acceleration inside SBGs and SFGs strongly motivated us to study their high-energy ray radiation using a one-zone stationary model. Here, NAIMA , a spectral research tool and references therein Zabalza2015 , provides a condition for us to complete this work. We used it to analyze the spectra of nine SBGs and four SFGs currently observed in this work. The next section presents the results of the Fermi Large Area Telescope ( Fermi LAT) data analysis, and Section 3 provides the best-fit results of target sources from one-zone and two-zone models. The discussion and conclusion are presented in Section 4 and 5, respectively. In this analysis, the time band of photon events was collected from 2008-08-04 15:43:46 to 2019-08-25 02:54:51. The energy range was from 100 MeV to 500 GeV. We used the Fermi Science Tools version v11r5p3 package http://fermi.gsfc.nasa.gov/ssc/data/analysis/software/ provided by the Fermi Science Support Center (FSSC). We chose the Pass 8 data (evclass = 128 evtype = 3) and the photon events with a zenith angle of 90 ^ to minimize the contribution from the Earth's limb. We used gtmktime to obtain high-quality data with good time intervals, using the expression of (DATA QUAL > 0) and (LAT CONFIG == 1) recommended by the Fermi team. The instrumental response function ``P8R3 SOURCE V2'' was adopted. To reduce the data, we followed the data analysis method provided by the Fermi Science Support Center //fermi.gsfc.nasa.gov/ssc/data/analysis/ . The photon events from a 20^ 20^ region of interest (ROI) were selected. The center coordinates listed in Table Tab1 were those of target sources from the Fermi Large Telescope Fourth Source Catalog 4FGL; 4FGL and SIMBAD simbad.u-strasbg.fr . We used the script make4FGLxml.py https://fermi.gsfc.nasa.gov/ssc/data/analysis/user/ and 4FGL to generate each source model file. Owing to a small data sets, we selected a power-law (PL) model as the spectral model of M31 and M33, based on Feng2019 and Xi2020 . For all SBGs, we chose the PL spectral model; for those of SMC and LMC, we selected the log parabola (LOG) model by referring to 4FGL. Using the binned maximum likelihood method, we fitted the photon events of all 4FGL sources within the 30 ^ range around each target source. Here, the two background templates, including Galactic ( gll iem v07.fits ) and extragalactic diffuse emissions ( iso P8R3 SOURCE V2 V1.txt ) http://fermi.gsfc.nasa.gov/ssc/data/access/lat/BackgroundModels.html , were added to each source model file, where their normalizations were set as free parameters. Within 5^ of the center of the ROI, the normalization and spectral index of each source were set as free parameters as well. Spectral Energy Distribution To derive the spectral energy distribution (SED) of each source, the energy range from 100 MeV to 500 GeV was divided into six equally spaced log10( E ) energy bins for Circinus, NGC 2146, NGC 3424, M31; for Arp 299 and M 33, we generated five energy bins for a low data statistic, referring to Xi2020 . For the SEDs of other sources, we chose to generate 10 energy bins. Because the statistics and TS values from the last three bins were less for NGC 253 and M82, we considered combining the three bins to one, based on Abdalla2018 . For each bin of all SEDs, we guaranteed that their TS values were greater than 4. For the energy bin with a TS value < 4, the upper limits of the 95 confidence level were given. All SEDs of each source are shown in Figure Fig1 . The global fit results for each source are given in Table Tab1 . We found that the average value of the power-law spectral index of SBGs is approximately 2.23, which is consistent with the maximum likelihood analysis results of Ackermann2012a and Ajello2020 . For each panel of Figure Fig1 , we provided the differential sensitivities of Large High Altitude Air Shower Observatory Bai2019 and the Cherenkov Telescope Array in the northern/southern hemisphere CTA2019 , to predict their likely very-high-energy (VHE) emission in the future. 1.5 Model Introduction The tool, NAIMA , provided a one-zone stationary model and the proton-proton (pp) interaction differential cross-section of PYTHIA 8 Kafexhiu2014 to fit the spectra of the target sources. This tool employs the Markov chain Monte Carlo method, implemented in the emcee package Foreman2013 . We will use NAIMA to complete all the following analyses. The high-energy radiations in SFGs and SBGs are thought to be caused by CRs colliding with the surrounding interstellar medium (ISM) resulting in the generation of neutral pions and other products Abdo2010b,Abdo2010d,Peretti2019 . In addition, the neutral pions can decay into high-energy ray photons Stecker1971,Dermer1986 ; thus, the SBGs and SFGs were considered to be possible ray sources in the local universe Dermer1986,Strong1976,Paglione1996,Blom1999,Domingo2005 . The ray radiation of SBGs and SFGs above 100 MeV is widely regarded to be of hadronic origin; whereas pionic decay is regarded as the dominant mechanism of ray generation inside them e.g., Domingo2005,Rephaeli2010,Tang2014,Abdalla2018,Peretti2019,Wang2018 . Therefore, we assumed that the high-energy radiation of SBGs and SFGs originated from the collective interaction of internal SNRs and molecular cloud gas inside those starburst regions, and that pionic decay is the dominant radiation mechanism for the GeV ray emission of the SBGs and SFGs. For the absorption of extragalactic background light (EBL), here we consider the EBL model from Dominguez2011 in all the following analyses. For SNRs in the Milky Way, the protonic energy distribution of a power-law distribution with an exponential cutoff (ECPL) has been widely used to explain their ray spectra Aharonian2006,Xing2016, Xin2019,Xiang2021a . Here, we assumed that the protonic energy distributions of SNRs within SBGs and SFGs satisfy the same formula, which is as follows: N(E) = N_ 0 ( E E_ 0 )^ exp (- E E_ cutoff , where N_ 0 is the amplitude, E is the particle energy, is the power-law spectral index, and E_ cutoff is the cutoff energy, and E_ 0 = 1 TeV. Model fit Diffusive shock acceleration (DSA), as a predominant acceleration mechanism, is generally used to explain high-energy particle acceleration at SNR shocks of up to approximately 100 TeV or even higher Aharonian2007,Aharonian2011,Morlino2012a . Therefore, here the range of protonic energy was selected from 0.1 GeV to 0.5 PeV e.g., Abdalla2018 . When the protonic energy distributions of the SBGs and SFGs satisfy (1), we used NAIMA to obtain the best-fit results of the SEDs of all sources, as shown in Figure Fig2 . The related best-fit parameters are given in Table Tab2 , where the protons energy budget W_ p was calculated to be above 290 MeV, which is considered as the pion production threshold Abdalla2018 . Except for the hardening spectral components from NGC 1068 and NGC 4945, we found that the one-zone model with ECPL can explain the SEDs of most SBGs and SFGs. However, we found that the first bins of SEDs of NGC 1068, M82, NGC 253, Arp 299, and SMC cannot be well explained; we assumed that the first bins may be from the contributions of the Bremsstrahlung emission and inverse Compton scattering of the primary or secondary electrons of CRs e.g., Tang2014 .

sources.summary: The review describes the SMC as a low-metallicity, relatively primitive Magellanic galaxy whose evolved-star populations and C/O-rich AGB ratios trace its older, metal-poor history.

quotes.quote: One example supporting this concept of the dependence on ambient metallicity is a deep search for OH/IR stars (O-rich AGB stars with cold CSE) that was performed by Goldman2018 in the Small Magellanic Cloud (SMC), a low metallicity galaxy. They made long integration on a dozen of luminous, long-period, large-amplitude variable stars and no one confirmed to be an O-rich star.

Surveys such as the Herschel inventory of the Agents of Galaxy Evolution (HERITAGE) Meixner2010 have allowed to investigate the presence of evolved stars candidates in the LMC and SMC with metallicites of 0.5 and 0.2 Z_ , respectively Russell1992,Jones2015 . Studies highlight the importance of using the ratios between C- and O-rich AGB stars to evaluate the metallicity distribution in galaxies like the Magellanic Clouds, unveiling that stars in the SMC are in general older and metal poorer than the LMC, in line with the fact that the SMC is considered as a more primitive and less evolved galaxy than the LMC Cioni2003,Cioni2006 .

sources.summary: The paper treats the SMC as a likely LMC satellite whose inferred accretion is earlier than the LMC, with its orbit sensitive to the LMC potential.

quotes.quote: According to Patel2020LMCSMC , long-term LMC satellites are the SMC, Carina II, Carina III, Horologium I and Hydrus I, while Phoenix II and Reticulum II are recently accreted by the LMC.

The other two likely LMC satellites, SMC and Horologium I, also have a likelihood peak at =1.5 Gyrs , however the NN predicts that they are more likely to have have been accreted earlier, around 8 Gyrs . We suspect this discrepancy is due to not including the LMC potential when modelling the MW, with the LMC thought to have been rather massive at infall Penarrubia2016,Cautun2019LMC and even today having a considerable total mass Garavito-Camargo2019,Erkal that can have a large impact on the motion of dwarfs close to the LMC such as the SMC and Horologium I e.g. see and also Erkal for example of Galactic streams Patel2020LMCSMC .

However, for the SMC and Horologium I, we find considerably earlier accretion times than the LMC, indicating that for dwarfs close to the LMC we cannot neglect the potential of this massive satellite.

sources.summary: The paper places the SMC among massive Milky Way satellites with distinctive abundance trends and a stellar mass comparable to GE/S but below the LMC.

quotes.quote: By and large, the locus occupied by GE/S stars in the Mg-Fe plane is somewhat similar to that where stars from the massive MW satellites LMC, SMC, and Sgr dSph are located see also Hasselquist2021 . The distributions however differ in important details. All three of the massive satellites show, at a given [ Fe/H ] , a positive change in the slope of the [ Mg/Fe ] [ Fe/H ] relation, whereby [ Mg/Fe ] starts increasing with increasing metallicity.

The contrast between the histories of star formation of GE/S and massive MW satellites, and in particular the LMC and SMC is interesting in light of the fact that the mass of GE/S, according to various studies, is of the order of a few to several times 10 ^8 M_ e.g., Lane2021,Deason2019,mackereth2019origin , which is comparable to that of the SMC ( 5 10^8 M_ ) and smaller than that of the LMC 1.5 10^9 M_ , mcconnachie2012observed .

sources.summary: The review describes the SMC as a gas-rich, actively star-forming dwarf satellite with inefficient star formation and enhanced metal-poor-star process abundances favoring delayed production sources.

quotes.quote: The Large and Small Magellanic Clouds (LMC, SMC) are the only gas-rich and actively star-forming dwarf galaxy satellites of the Milky Way. The most comprehensive recent study of the LMC and SMC is by Nidever2020 . They were unable to study process chemical evolution, but they did show that the LMC and SMC have an inefficient star formation history, such that it should be dominated by delayed-enrichment sources even at relatively low metallicity. This is consistent with the findings of Reggiani2021 , who measured process abundances of metal-poor stars ( 2.5 [ Fe/H ] 1.5 ) in the LMC and SMC, finding they were unusually enhanced and thus favoring delayed sources of process production.

sources.summary: The paper describes the SMC as a nearby dwarf irregular with many high-mass X-ray binaries, linking its Be-HMXB population to star formation, low metallicity, and LMC interactions.

quotes.quote: The SMC is a dwarf irregular galaxy orbiting the Milky Way at a known distance of 62 0.3 kpc Scowcroft_2016 . It hosts more than 120 known high-mass X-ray binaries (HMXBs) Haberl_2022 ; see and for overviews of X-ray binaries. The optical counterpart is usually a Be star, with only one system hosting a supergiant donor star, namely SMC X-1 Haberl_2016 . The large population of SMC Be-type HMXBs is ascribed to: (i) recent star formation Galache_2008 ; (ii) low metallicity Antoniou_2010 ; and (iii) recent tidal interactions with the Large Magellanic Cloud Gardiner_1996 .

sources.summary: The paper describes the Small Magellanic Cloud as a star-forming Milky Way satellite with a large HMXB population monitored weekly by S-CUBED.

quotes.quote: This newly-discovered system resides in the Small Magellanic Cloud (SMC), a satellite galaxy of the Milky Way that is well-documented to have a large population of HMXBs 2010Antoniou due to an unusually-active period of star formation that occurred in the SMC's recent past 2004Harris, 2014Rezaeikh . New SMC BeXRBs are still occasionally discovered 2023Maitra , indicating that a hidden population of previously-undetected systems still exists within the dwarf galaxy. In an effort identify X-ray outbursts and detect new sources, the Swift SMC Survey (S-CUBED) 2018Kennea has operated since 2016 to provide regular X-ray and UV monitoring of the SMC. This survey utilizes the Neil Gehrels Swift Observatory 2004Gehrels to tile the galaxy on a weekly cadence with 142 overlapping tiles and 1 minute spent on each tile.

sources.summary: The paper describes the SMC as a nearby gas-rich dwarf irregular companion of the LMC, with low metallicity and an elongated three-dimensional shape.

quotes.quote: The SMC has long been thought to be a satellite of the LMC due to its proximity. It is at around 62 kpc from the MW e.g. cionivdm00,hilditch05,graczyk14 and 20-25 kpc away from the LMC. The SMC is a gas rich dwarf irregular galaxy e.g. rubio93,staveley-smith98 , and features a low metallicity environment e.g. Choudhury18,grady21 . An attempt to reconstruct its 3D shape has been carried out using red clump stars and other standard candles and revealed that it is elongated about 15-30 kpc approximately in the east/north-east towards south-west direction e.g. subramanian-subramanian12,ripepi17 .

sources.summary: The paper measures Stromgren photometric reddening in two SMC fields, finding foreground E(B-V)=0.070, intrinsic Cloud reddening up to 0.21, and small-scale variation across the fields.

quotes.quote: The individual reddenings for B stars in two fields in the Small Magellanic Cloud (SMC) and two fields in the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC) are determined by means of Str o mgren uvby CCD photometry. In both LMC fields we find a foreground reddening of E(B-V) = 0.085 0.02 , and for the SMC fields we find E(B-V) = 0.070 0.02 . In addition to the foreground reddening we find contributions from reddening intrinsically in the Clouds up to E(B-V) 0.21 . The intrinsic contribution is not correlated with position within our 6 4 5 CCD fields but varies in an essentially random way.

sources.summary: The paper describes the SMC as an elliptical Magellanic Cloud, compares its AGB luminosity histogram with the LMC, and notes longer-period variables and TRGB selection effects.

quotes.quote: A clearly elliptical structure is seen in the SMC with a semi--major axis of about 3^ and a semi--minor axis of about 2^ .

The global shape of the AGB histogram differs between the Clouds in the sense that it is rather monotonically decreasing for increasing mag in the SMC and it resembles a bump, thus with a flatter top, in the LMC.

In the SMC there are sources with longer periods, this is because the MACHO light--curves cover a longer time range with respect to the EROS2 data. In addition there is a lack of SMC sources around the TRGB, this is probably a selection effect because these sources do have an emission at 7 and 12 micron.

sources.summary: The paper includes SMC cluster samples in its disruption-time comparison, finding the same disruption-law slope as other fields but the longest t_4 value among the studied locations.

quotes.quote: We demonstrate the method and derive the values of t_4 and from cluster samples in selected fields of four galaxies: M51, M33, SMC and the solar neighbourhood. The values of are the same within their uncertainties in the four galaxies with < >= 0.62 0.06 . However, the disruption time t_4 of clusters of 10^4 M_ is very different in the different locations. It is shortest in the inner region of M51 ( t_4 = 4 10^7 yrs) and highest in the SMC ( t_4=4 10^9 yrs).

sources.summary: The paper finds no clear radial C/M trend in the Small Magellanic Cloud, linking its clumpy ratio pattern to line-of-sight extent or a more complex star-formation history.

quotes.quote: Regions of different metallicity have been identified in the Magellanic Clouds by using the ratio between Asymptotic Giant Branch stars of spectral type C and M. In the Large Magellanic Cloud the ratio appears to decrease radially while in the Small Magellanic Cloud (SMC) there is no clear trend, reflecting either the large extension of the SMC along the line of sight or a more complex star formation history. The distribution of the C/M ratio is clumpy and corresponds to a spread in [ Fe/H ] of 0.75 dex in both Clouds. There is an indication of increasing C/M ratio, thus decreasing metallicity, towards the Bridge region connecting the two Clouds.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes SMC supernova remnants, deriving physical parameters, a lower average hydrogen density than the LMC, and average gas-phase abundances for several elements.

quotes.quote: We present a detailed X-ray spectral analysis of 13 supernova remnants (SNR) in the Small Magellanic Cloud (SMC).

Our results indicate that the average SMC hydrogen density is a factor of 6 lower as compared to the Large Magellanic Cloud. This has obvious implications for the evolution and luminosities of the SMC SNRs. We also estimate the average SMC gas phase abundances for the elements O, Ne, Mg, Si and Fe.

sources.summary: The paper treats SMC microlensing events as self-lensing, citing parallax events and the SMC's line-of-sight extension as reasons self-lensing dominates there.

quotes.quote: MACHOs (Massive Astrophysical Compact Halo Objects) have been detected since 1993 in microlensing experiments towards the Large and Small Magellanic Clouds (LMC and SMC) as well as towards the galactic center. Although the events detected towards the SMC seem to be a self-lensing phenomenon , a similar interpretation of all the events discovered towards the LMC looks unlikely .

Note that we do not take into account the informations from the two observed parallax events towards the SMC which imply that these events are most likely self-lensing. From this fact one cannot infer that also all LMC events are self-lensing events since it is known that the SMC is extended along the line of sight (so that microlensing is dominated by self-lensing) while there is little evidence that the LMC is similarly extended.

sources.summary: The paper uses metal-poor SMC Cepheids as a differential sample and derives an SMC distance modulus of 18.88 mag, largely independent of metallicity.

quotes.quote: We have applied the near-IR Barnes-Evans realization of the Baade-Wesselink method as calibrated by Fouqu e Gieren ( ) to five metal-poor Cepheids with periods between 13 and 17 days in the Small Magellanic Cloud as well as to a sample of 34 Galactic Cepheids to determine the effect of metallicity on the period-luminosity (P-L) relation.

We do this by comparing absolute magnitudes for five metal-poor Cepheids in the Small Magellanic Cloud ( =-0.7 ) with solar-metallicity Cepheids in the Milky Way. The SMC Cepheids are the most metal-poor Cepheids which we have access to and provide a lever-arm in metallicity which is twice as large as for Cepheids in the LMC.
From our SMC Cepheid distances we determine the SMC distance to be 18.88 0.13 mag irrespective of metallicity.

sources.summary: The paper models SMC TRGB distances alongside the LMC, finding young RGB populations relative to globular-cluster calibrators and corrections to the SMC-LMC distance modulus of up to +0.10 mag.

quotes.quote: Here we have investigated the reliability of the widely used I band Tip of the Red Giant Branch (TRGB) relative distances for a sample of Local Group galaxies with complex Star Formation Histories (SFRs) and Age Metallicity Relationships (AMRs) namely the LMC, SMC and LGS3. The use of the K band is also discussed. By employing theoretical stellar population synthesis techniques, we find that using actual determinations of SFR and AMR of the LMC and SMC, their RGB is populated by stars much younger (by 9 Gyr) than the Galactic globular cluster counterparts, on which the I band (and K band) TRGB absolute magnitude is calibrated. This age difference induces a bias in both the photometric metallicity estimates based on the comparison of RGB colours with globular cluster ones, and the TRGB distances. The extent of the distance bias -- which is not influenced by the actual value of the TRGB absolute magnitude zero point -- is strongly dependent on the specific TRGB technique applied, and on the assumed I band bolometric correction ( BC_I ) scale adopted; the correction to apply to the SMC--LMC distance modulus ranges from 0 mag up to +0.10 mag.

sources.summary: The review describes EGRET non-detection of the SMC, setting a gamma-ray upper limit that argues against a universal cosmic-ray density across galaxies.

quotes.quote: The Small Magellanic Cloud (SMC) was also observed by EGRET, but no detection was found. An upper limit of 0.5 10 ^ 7 photons cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 was set for the ray emission above 100 MeV (Sreekumar et al. 1993). If the cosmic ray density in the SMC were as high as it is in our Galaxy, the flux in rays would be 2.4 10 ^ 7 photons cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 , a level incompatible with the experimental result. It was then the non-detection of the SMC what defined that the distribution of cosmic rays is galactic in origin, local to accelerators and thus enhanced in regions of star formation rather than universal.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes one SMC carbon star, describing the SMC as an irregular galaxy at about 63 kpc with low mean metallicity confirmed by BMB-B 30.

quotes.quote: In this first study we present the spectroscopic results for three carbon stars, one belonging to the Small Magellanic Cloud (SMC) and two stars to the Sagittarius dwarf spheroidal galaxy (Sgr dSph). Table 1 shows the photometric properties of our programme stars. The SMC is an irregular galaxy at a distance of about 63 kpc (Cioni et al. ) and suffering an interstellar extinction A_ B =0.16^ mag (NASA/IPAC database, Schlegel et al. ). Its mean metallicity is around [ M/H ] =-0.74 Here we use the standard notation for the chemical abundance ratio of any element X, [ X/H ] = log (X/H) _ log(X/H) _ where log (H) 12 is the abundance of hydrogen by number.
In particular, the metallicity derived for BMB-B 30, belonging to the SMC, confirms the low values previously reported by Rolleston et al. (1999) as the mean metallicity for this galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper describes SMC distance measurements from eclipsing binaries, saying the binary-based results agree with other distance indicators and giving a distance modulus derived from fifty eclipsing binaries.

quotes.quote: A parallel analysis of binaries in the Small Magellanic Cloud has been undertaken by hhh03 . In both cases the binary-based results are in excellent agreement with the determinations by other means and have an accuracy comparable to that of the best indicators. The latest estimate for the LMC, according to gui05 is (m-M)_0=18.42 0.07 , in good agreement with the value to which the various other results seem to converge (the weighted average from different methods, according to alv04 is 18.50 0.02 ). Similarly, the SMC distance modulus, as obtained by hhh03,hhh05 from fifty EBs, is (m-M)_0=18.91 0.03 0.1 (the last term being an evaluation of systematic errors). The value is in between the `` long" distance derived from Cepheids see, e.g., bono01 and the ``short" one from red clump stars e.g twa99 .

sources.summary: The SMC is discussed as the LMC's interacting companion; a close encounter about 4 Gyr ago is tied to renewed LMC globular cluster formation and the age gap.

quotes.quote: B04 and Bekki Chiba (2005; ) challenged this age gap problem by investigating chemodynamical evolution of the LMC interacting both with the Galaxy and the SMC for a long time scale ( 9 Gyr). They found that the first close encounter between the LMC and the SMC about 4 Gyr ago was the beginning of a period of strong tidal interaction witch likely induced dramatic gas cloud collisions, leading to an enhancement of the GC formation which has been sustained by strong tidal interactions to the present day. Figure 3, showing the simulated age distributions of field stars and GCs in a model, reveals that GC formation can be reactivated about 3-4 Gyr ago, when the LMC can start its strong tidal interaction with the SMC.
These results imply that the origin of the age gap can be closely associated with interaction histories of the LMC, the SMC, and the Galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper describes SMC planetary nebulae as a low-metallicity comparison sample, with sparse ultraviolet carbon data and strong oxygen depletion in nitrogen-rich type I nebulae.

quotes.quote: The SMC is also poorly sampled, with only 12 IUE spectra for 60 SMC PN known [ 16 ] . We have obtained 12 ACS prism spectra and were able to determine the carbon lines. Our work on SMC PN carbon determination is in preparation.
It is remarkable how the oxygen gets depleted in nitrogen rich type I SMC PN, while the effect is much milder in the LMC PN.

sources.summary: The paper says VMC covers the SMC, including optical follow-up, and reports a metal-rich ring plus an anticlockwise metallicity progression tied to dynamical evolution or star formation.

quotes.quote: These data will cover entirely the major components of the system (Fig.  vmc ): a wide area comprising the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC), the Small Magellanic Cloud (SMC), the Bridge connecting them and a few fields in the Stream where the stellar content is expected to be large.
Both the Bridge and SMC areas will also be covered at optical wavelengths by the VLT Survey Telescope (VST).

The SMC stellar population appears more metal rich in a ring surrounding the central part of the galaxy. A region of increasing metallicity moves anti-clockwise with increasing time perhaps tracing the dynamical evolution of the galaxy and/or the propagation of star formation in agreement with the results suggested by .

sources.summary: The paper includes the SMC in the FIR dust-colour analysis, noting proposed silicate-dominated dust and that its FIR colours lie on the same main correlation as the Galaxy and LMC.

quotes.quote: According to pei92 , the ratio between those two species is different among the Galaxy, the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC) and the Small Magellanic Cloud (SMC). In the SMC, pei92 suggest a silicate-dominated dust composition, while welty01 argue against such a composition from a study of the depletion pattern but see li06 .
The main correlation also explains the FIR colours of the LMC and the SMC (H06b).

It is surprising that the FIR colours of the LMC and the SMC are located on the main correlation defined by the Galactic dust grains, and it is interesting to give a unified understanding of the FIR colours for those three galaxies.

sources.summary: The paper describes the SMC as having an age-dependent metallicity pattern, including a southeastern high-metallicity region and a clockwise temporal shift leaving a metal-poor center.

quotes.quote: The SMC mean age and metallicity The distribution of metallicity across the SMC as a function of age shows a very interesting pattern, perhaps associated with the propagation of star formation throughout time. A region of high metallicity is located South-East of the galaxy centre and corresponds to a mean age of 2 Gyr. Going back in time this region moves clockwise to the West, leaving the centre of the galaxy metal poor and with very little variation smc , Fig. smc .

sources.summary: The paper cites Small Magellanic Cloud H I shell studies where 509 young shells were cross-correlated with stellar objects, 59 lacked young counterparts, and turbulence was proposed as an origin.

quotes.quote: Hatzidimitriou et al. (2005) cross-correlated the positions of 509 young neutral hydrogen shells detected in the Small Magellanic Cloud (SMC) with the locations of known OB-associations, Wolf-Rayet stars, supernova remnants and stellar clusters. They found that 59 shells have no young stellar objects associated with them despite the distributions of their radii and expansion velocities are consistent with those predicted by the multiple SNe model. They concluded that turbulence may be a promising mechanism that would allow us to understand the origin of these objects, but a quantitative comparison of the existing theory with observations is not possible at this moment.

sources.summary: The paper studies Be/X-ray binaries in the Small Magellanic Cloud, emphasizing its unusually rich HMXB population, XMM-Newton observations, and absorption correlated with the SMC column density.

quotes.quote: We analysed eight XMM-Newton observations toward the Small Magellanic Cloud (SMC), performed between October 2006 and June 2007, to investigate high mass X-ray binary systems.
Together with the Milky Way, the Small Magellanic Cloud (SMC) is the galaxy with the highest number of known high mass X-ray binaries (HMXBs).
An overall correlation of the absorption with the total SMC \ column density suggests that the absorption seen in the X-ray spectra is often largely caused by interstellar gas.

sources.summary: The paper finds no significant SMC metallicity gradient, links a 3 Gyr star-formation episode to LMC interaction, and connects its abundance to the Magellanic Bridge.

quotes.quote: In the SMC, [ Fe/H ] has a constant value of 1.25 0.01 dex up to 12 kpc.

The metallicity of the SMC supports star formation, 3 Gyr ago, as triggered by LMC interaction and sustained by the bar in the outer region of the galaxy. The SMC [ Fe/H ] agrees with the present-day abundance in the Bridge and shows no significant gradient.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes SMC extended structures alongside the LMC, finding hierarchical young stellar substructure and older clusters mixed by crossing-time dynamics.

quotes.quote: Similar conclusions apply to the SMC, in which stars appear to have formed with a high degree of (fractal) sub-structure, possibly imprinted by the turbulent nature of the parent gas; these structures are subsequently erased by random motions in the galactic potential on a time-scale of a crossing time through the galaxy ( GBE08 ). In this paper we investigate the degree of spatial correlation among the different kinds of LMC and SMC extended structures listed in the updated catalogue of UpCat , together with its relation to star formation. We also study properties of their size distribution functions. Only two wide-apart age ranges are used for spatial correlation purposes: (i) very young objects (not older than 20 Myr, and probably younger than 10 Myr), which encompass clusters related to nebular emission and associations related or not to emission, as classified and catalogued from sky survey plates by UpCat and references therein, and (ii) old clusters (older than 600 Myr). According to our definition, the dynamical age of the very young clusters is lower (Sect. 2pcf ) than the crossing time (of the host galaxy), while for the old ones it corresponds to several crossing times, which is important for interpreting the spatial correlation in different time periods.

sources.summary: The paper treats the SMC orbit independently from the LMC, notes uncertain modeling of LMC-SMC interactions, and cites lower-mass Milky Way results implying first infall with the LMC.

quotes.quote: We do not model the LMC and SMC together as is often done in the literature e.g. 2007ApJ...668..949B,2008ApJ...684L..87B , but investigate instead their orbits independently of each other. As shown in sec:3D , our model systematics are too large to determine whether or not it is better to include LMC-SMC interactions in our models. Notice, that we only find bound orbits for the LMC because of the large Milky Way masses in our models. Recent results for LMC orbits in a lower MW mass potential by 2006ApJ...652.1213K and 2007ApJ...668..949B suggest that the LMC (and with it the SMC) are falling into the Milky Way for the first time.

sources.summary: The paper studies the SMC as a Magellanic Cloud galaxy, reporting a large massive-star catalog and higher Oe/Be fractions and luminosities than the LMC.

quotes.quote: We present a study of the infrared properties of 4922 spectroscopically confirmed massive stars in the Large and Small Magellanic Clouds, focusing on the active OB star population.

We detect a distinct Be star sequence, displaced to the red, and find a higher fraction of Oe and Be stars among O and early-B stars in the SMC, respectively, when compared to the LMC, and that the SMC Be stars occur at higher luminosities.
The SMC spectral type catalog contains 5324 massive stars; 3654 of these are included in the photometric catalog, for which uniform photometry from 0.3-24 m is presented in Paper II.

sources.summary: The paper treats the Small Magellanic Cloud as a nearby low-metallicity star-forming galaxy and studies how star formation propagated through its NGC 346 region and field population.

quotes.quote: The Small Magellanic Cloud (SMC) is the closest star forming galaxy with a low metallicity ( Z=0.004 ) typical of late-type dwarfs and most similar to that of primordial galaxies. For this reason an increasing number of studies are being devoted to its star formation history (SFH) and related processes. This research is part of a long-term project aimed at studying how the star formation started and propagated in the SMC, studying both young clusters and the field population.

sources.summary: The paper says the SMC is much more massive than typical modeled dwarfs, so it can retain warm gas against ram-pressure stripping, with tidal interactions potentially removing material.

quotes.quote: Under our model, the LMC and SMC being much more massive than the typical dwarf--- M_ LMC 2 10^ 10 M _ Schommer1992 , M_ SMC 3 10^9 M _ Harris2006 --will be able to retain significant quantities of warm gas against ram-pressure stripping. This protection from stripping would allow a significant quantity of gas to survive until the present day, with only tidal interactions---potentially those between the LMC and SMC---removing material from either dwarf galaxy. Although tidal stripping is included in our model, it is too simplistic to model the multi-body system present in the Magellanic clouds, with hydrodynamical simulations required to simulate the LMC-SMC-Galaxy system Lin1995,JBH2007,Nidever2008,Besla2010 .

sources.summary: The paper says the SMC has velocity near the Milky Way escape speed, has been modeled with the LMC as a first-passage satellite, and may be unbound.

quotes.quote: In addition, recent proper motion measurements of the Small and Large Magellanic Clouds (SMC/LMC) ( kallivayalil06a ; kallivayalil06b ) suggest that the total velocities of these clouds approach the escape speed of the Milky Way. showed that under the assumption of a Milky Way with virial mass M_ vir 10^ 12 M_ , the LMC and SMC are on their first passage about the Milky Way. The current results seem to increase the likelihood that the SMC/LMC are unbound.

sources.summary: The paper includes the SMC in the Local Group gamma-ray population study and contrasts its steepening spectrum with harder LAT-detected starburst spectra.

quotes.quote: A study of the Local Group galaxies detected at GeV energies, including the Milky Way (MW), Small SMC, lat_2010_smc and Large Magellanic Clouds LMC, lat_2010_lmc , and M31, identified a simple power law relation between star-formation and gamma-ray luminosity lat_2010_local_group . Five Local Group galaxies previously studied in LAT data, the SMC, LMC, Milky Way, M31, and M33, are additionally included in a population study of low-redshift star-forming galaxies. Whereas the average photon index of the four LAT-detected starburst galaxies is 2.2 extending to TeV energies, the spectra of the LMC and SMC appear to steepen above 2 GeV lat_2010_smc,lat_2010_lmc .

sources.summary: The paper models the SMC TP-AGB stellar population with CB* simulations, using the SMC star formation history and chemical evolution to compare predicted luminosity/color distributions with observations.

quotes.quote: We show that Montecarlo simulations of the TP-AGB stellar population in the LMC and SMC galaxies using the CB ^ * models produce LF and color distributions that are in closer agreement with observations than those obtained with the BC03 and CB07 models.

Using the same procedure and the SFH of the SMC from we model the TP-AGB stellar population in the SMC galaxy, Fig. fig4 . In the case of the SMC the chemical evolution indicated by is included in our simulations.

sources.summary: The paper estimates SMC diffuse FUV emission from UIT/FUSE fields, reports regional variation tied to stellar content, and finds 34-44 percent of the FUSE-band emission escaping as diffuse light.

quotes.quote: Similarly, the UIT fields in the SMC used for the calculation of FUV diffuse fraction are: NGC 346, NGC 330, NGC 292 and NGC 267. The diffuse emission coming out of these regions shows a large regional variation due to their defference in the stellar contents. The diffuse FUV fraction escaping out of the SMC ranges between 34 and 44 in the em FUSE bands (1000 -- 1150 ) with a further increase upto 63 at 1615 .

sources.summary: The paper frames SMC star clusters as probes of the galaxy's chemical and dynamical evolution, inhomogeneous chemical enrichment, and inner age-metallicity gradients.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: We adopt the coordinates of the SMC centre from , ( , ) = ( 0^ h 52^ m 45^ s , 72^ 49 43 ), and a minor to major axis ratio of b/a =1/2 .

quotes.quote: Received ; accepted Stellar clusters in the Small Magellanic Cloud (SMC) are useful probes for studying the chemical and dynamical evolution of this neighbouring dwarf galaxy, enabling inspection of a large period covering over 10 Gyr.

This suggests that, after an initial period of rapid enrichment, that brought the metallicity up to around [ Fe/H ] 1.2, the SMC chemical enrichment is very inhomogeneous, since there is a spread in metallicity from 11 to 3 Gyr, with a trend for slow enrichment.

A gradient of age and metallicity can be found in the inner part of the SMC (a < 2^ ), but in the outer regions the high dispersion in ages and metallicities prevents the identification of any trends.

sources.summary: The paper compares SMC dust production with AGB yield models, finding carbon-star yields are overpredicted and AGB stars may supply part of its dust mass.

quotes.quote: We compare theoretical dust yields for stars with mass 1 M_ m_ star 8 M_ , and metallicities 0.001 Z 0.008 with observed dust production rates (DPR) by carbon-rich and oxygen-rich Asymptotic Giant Branch (C-AGB and O-AGB) stars in the Large and Small Magellanic Clouds (LMC, SMC). The measured DPR of C-AGB in the LMC are reproduced only if the mass loss from AGB stars is very efficient during the carbon-star stage. The same yields over-predict the observed DPR in the SMC, suggesting a stronger metallicity dependence of the mass-loss rates during the carbon-star stage. DPR of O-AGB stars suggest that rapid silicate dust enrichment occurs due to efficient hot-bottom-burning if m_ star 3 M_ and Z 0.001 . When compared to the most recent observations, our models support a stellar origin for the existing dust mass, if no significant destruction in the ISM occurs, with a contribution from AGB stars of 70 in the LMC and 15 in the SMC.

sources.summary: The paper includes the SMC in N-body Magellanic-system models, constraining its LMC interactions and adopting rotation and mass parameters for its initial state.

quotes.quote: Later, they extended this analysis to include the Small Magellanic Cloud (SMC), in order to explain the formation of the Magellanic Stream and the Leading Arm 2010ApJ...721L..97B,2012MNRAS.421.2109B .

explore a wide range of orbital models and use a multi-component N-body representation for only the SMC, in order to investigate the tidal effects on its disc and the formation of the main structures in the system.
The total rotation curve peaks around 64 km/s, with the total mass within 3 kpc equal to 2.8 10^9 M _ 2004ApJ...604..176S .

sources.summary: The review uses the SMC to illustrate Local Group central-satellite failures, arguing its evolution is tied to the Milky Way halo rather than to M31.

quotes.quote: We have to consider that the relationship of the Milky Way to M31 cannot be the considered on the same grounds as the relationship of galaxies such as the LMC and SMC to it. The situation would be worse if the Milky Way is assigned as a satellite of M31. Then, all of the Milky Way's satellites would also be assigned to be satellites of M31. Herein lies a potentially significant problem, as it is clear that the evolution of the LMC and SMC must be more governed by the halo of the Milky Way, not M31, and this is lost in the central/satellite framework.

The concern here is that we may be losing our ability to understanding the physics of galaxy evolution in systems similar to the Local Group if we are assigning the SMC and LMC satellites of M31, a galaxy that clearly does not dominate their future evolution.

sources.summary: The paper predicts the SMC progenitor had z=7 UV luminosity around 13.8, contributes to reionization models, and would be observable at z=2.

quotes.quote: The Small Magellanic Cloud (SMC) and IC 1613 likely had (z=7) 13.8 ; virtually all reionization models therefore require progenitors of such galaxies to contribute to reionization.

The LMC and SMC are predicted to have (z 2) 16 and 14 , respectively, placing them well within the reach of \ at z 2 . IC 1613 and the Fornax dSph are predicted to have (z 2) 12.5 , meaning the progenitors of these galaxies are just at the edge of 's current capabilities at z=2 .
