Other Local Group galaxies in our sample are all predicted to be fainter than =-12 at z 2 .

Using the modeled UV luminosities, we have shown that Local Group galaxies such as the Fornax dSph and IC 1613 were, at z 2 , slightly less luminous ( 12.5 ) than the faintest galaxies \ is currently observing at that epoch ( 13 ). The LMC and SMC are predicted to be substantially more luminous at that time; many of the faint ( 15 ) galaxies observed at z 2 may evolve to become Magellanic irregulars in the local Universe.

sources.summary: The paper discusses the SMC as the Bridge's western/foreground anchor, whose interaction with the LMC shaped Bridge clusters, SMC Wing structure, and tidal dwarf candidates.

quotes.quote: firstpage - lastpage We obtained SOAR telescope B and V photometry of 14 star clusters and 2 associations in the Bridge tidal structure connecting the LMC and SMC.
The derived distance moduli for the Bridge objects ( 1^h56^m< <2^h28^m ) suggests that the Bridge is a structure connecting the LMC far-side in the East to the foreground of the SMC to the West.

The dynamical evolution of the Clouds has been addressed by N-body simulations ( BC05 , B+12 , Kalil13 ) showing that a flat, old LMC disk would have evolved to a bar, thick disk and halo, through interactions with the SMC.

sources.summary: The paper identifies the SMC as a Magellanic satellite galaxy, reports 8,401 OGLE eclipsing binaries in it, and finds the systems group around the SMC center.

quotes.quote: The Magellanic System, composed of the Large (LMC) and Small Magellanic Clouds (SMC) -- two satellite galaxies of the Milky Way -- is a perfect environment for the stellar variability studies.
In this work, we present the OGLE collection of binary stars in the Magellanic System, containing 48 605 objects including 40 204 systems in the LMC, and 8 401 in the SMC.

While identified eclipsing binaries are likely to be a mixture of populations, they seem to trace the bar and spiral arms of the LMC and group around the center of SMC, which resembles more the distribution of the young population (Soszy n ski 2015) than the old one (Soszy n ski 2016).

sources.summary: The paper uses the SMC as a low-metallicity galaxy environment, linking Wing OB-star spectroscopy to metallicity, stellar evolution paths, and stochastic massive-star formation.

quotes.quote: Our sample stars belong to the Wing of the SMC, which is the nearest (DM =18.7 mag) Cignoni2009 low density Stanimirovic2000 and low reddening region at low metallicity.
The quantitative spectroscopy of OB star populations in the SMC allows us to test massive star evolution, their feedback, and star formation in low-metallicity galaxies.

The ages and spatial distribution of massive stars in the SMC Wing shows that star formation is proceeding in this quiescent low density region since more than 100 Myr, and suggests that massive star formation is stochastic there and not spatially progressing.

sources.summary: The paper treats the SMC as the previously known and relatively bright LMC-system member, using it in the inferred satellite mass function.

quotes.quote: Recent Gaia proper motions confirm new members of the LMC-system in addition to the previously known SMC, including two classical dwarf galaxies ( > 10^5 ; Carina and Fornax) as well as several ultra-faint dwarfs (Car2, Car3, Hor1, and Hyd1).

This criteria was used in sales2011MagGal to conclude that, with the exception of the SMC, no other classical dwarf with available proper motions at that time was consistent with an LMC association. With the arrival of new Gaia DR2 data, proper motions are now available for many dwarfs in the MW, including classical and ultrafaint dwarfs (we assume the cutoff for ultrafaints to be a stellar mass of < 10^5 , as in bullock2017lcdm ). With the new 6D information, kallivayalil2018MCsats confirmed a likely association for 4 ultrafaints: Car2, Car3, Hor1 and Hyd1.

With stellar masses of 3.8 5 and 2.0 7 , respectively, this newly confirmed pair of galaxies fills the classical dwarf scale (10 ^5 10 ^7 ) in the satellite mass function of the LMC, which was previously populated only by ultrafaint dwarfs (Car2, Car3, Hor1, Hyd1 aside from the from the relatively bright SMC with 4.6 8 ).

sources.summary: The review describes SMC extinction curves as especially steep in the UV while noting sightline diversity, including the SMC Wing's stronger bump and shallower UV slope.

quotes.quote: Early studies by Nandy1981, Koornneef1981,Clayton1985 and Fitzpatrick1985 found that, while there was significant regional variation in the curve across the LMC, on average the LMC had a UV curve steeper than the Milky Way's, especially in the 30 Doradus star-forming complex. Similarly, early studies of the SMC e.g., Prevot1984,Gordon1998 found even steeper UV slopes. In short, while the optical extinction curve slopes for the LMC and the SMC fall within the range of MW sightlines, the UV slopes tend to be significantly steeper (Figure fig:ext b), highlighting the limitation of characterizing curve slopes with R_V alone.

Likewise, the extinction curve toward SMC Wing, a more quiescent and gas-poor sightline, has a bump as strong as many MW sightlines and a relatively shallow UV slope (a number of other sightlines in the SMC show evidence for a weak UV bump, albeit in attenuation curves; Hagen2017 ).

sources.summary: The paper uses Magellanic Cloud massive-star counts to estimate SMC B/R and WR/RSG ratios, notes model metallicity mismatches, and contrasts the lower-metallicity SMC with the LMC in inferred natal binary or rotation fractions.

quotes.quote: For both the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC, plotted in orange) and Small Magellanic Cloud (SMC, plotted in blue), we estimate R _ B/R using data from massey03 and R _ WR/RSG from massey03 , neugent11 , and neugent12 , and find R _ B/R = 13.521 ^ +0.937 _ 0.865 , R _ WR/RSG = 0.659 ^ +0.071 _ 0.064 in the LMC, and R _ B/R = 16.449 ^ +1.898 _ 1.658 , R _ WR/RSG = 0.134 ^ +0.046 _ 0.036 in the SMC. The Geneva models fail to reproduce the observations, while the BPASS models underpredict the metallicity of both clouds ( Z 0.001/0.002 for the SMC/LMC respectively). In both cases, the data indicate that the initial f_ bin or f_ rot is larger in the higher metallicity LMC than in the lower metallicity SMC, consistent with the results in dornwallenstein18 .

sources.summary: The paper discusses the SMC as the LMC interaction partner, a likely source of stripped Bridge gas, and a contributor to Magellanic ionizing escape fractions.

quotes.quote: Recent kinematic studies and simulations Zivick2019,Oey2018 support a direct collision scenario between the LMC and the SMC.

If the Bridge of gas connecting the Magellanic Clouds formed from stripped SMC gas during the latest encounter, chemical abundances of the young Bridge population must be compatible with that of the SMC.
Based on this assumption we estimate a lower limit for the escape fractions from the LMC and the SMC to be 1 - 2 and, 4 - 7 , respectively.

sources.summary: The paper describes the Small Magellanic Cloud as more distant and less massive than the LMC, probably tidally stripped, with a plausible mass range that yields a small acceleration contribution.

quotes.quote: The Small Magellanic Cloud is slightly more distant ( 62.8 2.5 ; 2000A&A...359..601C ), and significantly less massive. It is thought that it has been significantly tidally stripped by the LMC (e.g. \ 2020MNRAS.495...98D ), so its mass is likely to be substantially lower than its estimated peak mass of 7 10^ 10 M_ (e.g. \ 2019MNRAS.487.5799R ), but is hard to determine based on dynamical modelling. We follow 2020ApJ...893..121P and consider the range of possible masses (0.5 3) 10^ 10 M_ , which gives an acceleration of 0.005 to 0.037 .

sources.summary: The review describes Gaia variable-star work as tracing the SMC with RR Lyrae candidates and revealing old- and young-star bridges between the SMC and LMC.

quotes.quote: By calibrating against samples of known variable stars were able to identify candidate RR Lyrae stars in a field covering the Magellanic clouds. These candidate RR Lyrae beautifully outline the LMC and SMC and in particular reveal the bridge of old stars between the two Milky Way companions. A combination of 1 with GALEX data revealed the existence of a bridge of younger stars, offset from the bridge of old stars and coincident with the known HI bridge between the LMC and SMC.

sources.summary: The paper describes the SMC as a nearby low-absorption laboratory for hot-ISM and SNR-population work, noting joint LMC/SMC surveys and one very low-luminosity SNR detection.

quotes.quote: The two largest satellite galaxies of the Milky Way, Large and Small Magellanic Cloud (LMC, SMC), with their low foreground absorption and small distances of 50 kpc and 60 kpc, respectively, are the ideal laboratories for exploring the global structures of the ISM in a galaxy. Studies of different types of SNRs and a systematic study of the SNR populations in the LMC and SMC have been performed in radio, optical, and X-rays e.g., 2016A&A...585A.162M,2017ApJS..230....2B,2021MNRAS.500.2336Y , yielding 60 confirmed SNRs in the LMC and many more candidates.

So far, only 3 SNRs have been detected with a luminosity below 10 ^ 34 erg/s in the LMC and one in the SMC, while both Magellanic Clouds are the only galaxies in which we can probe the X-ray emission of SNRs down to the lowest luminosities.

sources.summary: The paper treats SMC as a nearby dust-attenuation case study: Swift/UVOT work found a steep median attenuation curve with a sub-Milky-Way UV bump and regional variations tied to higher A_ V.

quotes.quote: We note that the Swift /UVOT data have been used to study the spatial resolved dust attenuation curve and UV bump for a handful of nearby galaxies, e.g. M81 and Holmberg IX by Hoversten-2011 , SMC by Hagen2017 and NGC 628 by Decleir2019 . Both M81 and Holmberg IX can be best fit with a Milky Way extinction curve with a prominent UV bump, while the median attenuation curve of NGC 628 and SMC is fairly steep, with a sub-MW-type UV bump. In addition, intriguing variations are observed among different regions in NGC 628 and SMC, with regions of higher A_ V found to have shallower attenuation curves and weaker UV bumps.

sources.summary: The paper treats HW 42 as an SMC cluster whose revised age affects the SMC star-formation history, composite field population, and age-metallicity relation for star clusters.

quotes.quote: I revisited the age of the Small Magellanic Cloud cluster HW 42, whose previous estimates differ in more than 6 Gyr, thus challenging the most updated knowledge of the SMC star formation history.

I conclude that HW 42 is a 6.2 ^ +1.6 _ 1.3 Gyr old ( [ Fe/H ] = -0.89 ^ +0.10 _ 0.11 dex) SMC cluster projected on to a SMC composite star field population which shows variations in magnitude, color, and stellar density of Main Sequence stars. The present outcome solves the conundrum of the previous age discrepancies and moves HW 42 to a region in the SMC age-metallicity relationship populated by star clusters.

sources.summary: The paper compares the SMC to the LMC as a carbon-star distance calibrator, emphasizing its more symmetric luminosity function and near-identical median and mode magnitudes.

quotes.quote: The parameters obtained for the LMC and SMC show a significant difference between the shapes of the luminosity functions of the CS in these two galaxies. The LMC is skewed towards brighter magnitudes while the SMC is fairly symmetric. The difference in symmetry is also noticeable in the difference between the value of the mode and the median M_J magnitude: in the LMC these two values differ by almost a tenth of a magnitude while in the SMC the values of the median and the mode are almost identical.

sources.summary: The review cites Fermi observations of the Small Magellanic Cloud showing extended low-energy gamma-ray emission, likely hadronic, and cosmic rays escaping below the calorimetric limit.

quotes.quote: By analyzing Fermi ray data of the Small Magellanic Cloud, lopez_evidence_2018 detect extended ray emission below 13 GeV along the ``bar'' and ``wing'' structure that are associated with regions of active star formation. While the contribution of pulsars is not expected to exceed 10 , most of the emission is likely due to hadronic CR ion interactions with the ISM in line with findings by persic_diffuse_2022 . A comparison to the star formation rate shows that the Small Magellanic Cloud is well below the ``calorimetric limit,'' implying that CRs are escaping this dwarf irregular galaxy via a combination of advection, streaming and/or diffusion.

sources.summary: The paper describes the SMC as an interacting Magellanic Cloud with elongated triaxial structure, measured metallicity, and likely dominant material contribution to the Bridge.

quotes.quote: The SMC is located at a distance of 62.4 0.8 kpc 2020ApJ...904...13G and, due to strong interactions with the LMC, presents an elongated, triaxial structure, with a line-of-sight depth of up to 14 kpc in the inner regions 2012ApJ...744..128S and 23 kpc in the eastern part 2013ApJ...779..145N .
The SMC has a mean metallicity of [ Fe/H ] =-0.99 0.01 dex 2014MNRAS.442.1680D and 0.9 0.2 dex 2016AJ....152...58P , derived from cluster and field red giants also using CaT lines.

These values are closer to the SMC metallicity than to that of the LMC, suggesting that the Bridge may have been formed mostly from SMC material, even though radial velocity measurements indicate that LMC gas might be present as well 1986PASA....6..471M .

sources.summary: The paper models the Small Magellanic Cloud as a low-mass galaxy whose delayed star-formation history peaks at 4.7 Gyr, while the power-law model keeps it in the increasing-SFH group.

quotes.quote: The less massive Small Magellanic Cloud (SMC) and M32 galaxy with a stellar mass of 4.2 10^8 M_ and 7.4 10^8 M_ , respectively, have also first an increasing and later on a decreasing SFH. The SMC has a maximal SFR of 0.05 M_ yr^ 1 at a lookback time of 4.7 Gyr , which is about 1.1 times higher than the present-day value. M32 has an SFR peak of 0.3 M_ yr^ 1 at a lookback time of 12.4 Gyr and a present-day SFR of only 1.3 10^ 4 M_ yr^ 1 . The Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC) with M_ * = 1.6 10^ 9 M_ and M33 with M_ * = 2.5 10^ 9 M_ have continuously rising SFHs in both the delayed- and power-law- SFH model. In the power-law- SFH model, the massive galaxies MW, M31, NGC 5128/Cen A, M81, IC 342, but also M32 have a decreasing, while the low-mass galaxies SMC, LMC, and M33 have an increasing SFH over cosmic time in agreement with e.g.

sources.summary: The paper applies GCVD to the SMC using the old cluster NGC 121, correcting its velocity dispersion and adopting cluster magnitude and extinction values.

quotes.quote: SMC We took central velocity dispersion data for the single old GC NGC 121 from . We correct this value to a global dispersion as explained in Section afiducialrelation . The V band magnitude of the cluster was taken from , and we used extinction values from .

sources.summary: The paper contrasts the SMC with the LMC, arguing that its stars lack a high-extinction tail and that its low luminosity and kinematics place it below the dust-lane transition.

quotes.quote: In contrast, comparable stars in the SMC show no such high extinction tail, suggesting that there is no dust lane blocking the view of the more distant stars (Zaritsky et al. \ 2002). The SMC is a very low mass galaxy, based on its luminosity. Although it has no observed rotation along the line of sight due to its face-on orientation, it has a central velocity dispersion of _v=21 (Hatzidimitiou et al. \ 1997) and a Tully-Fisher based rotation speed that clearly puts it on the low mass side of the V_c=120 transition.

sources.summary: The paper presents an SMC hot-star spectral catalogue, motivated by the galaxy's low metallicity and using 2dF observations to study its massive-star population and IMF.

quotes.quote: The Small Magellanic Cloud (SMC) is of particular interest as the nearest system with a well-established, substantial (factor 5) underabundance of metallic elements compared to those found in the Milky Way. This makes it an attractive target for investigating the role of metallicity in star formation and evolution, as well as a variety of other topics in stellar and galactic astrophysics. With this in mind, we have undertaken a spectroscopic survey of the SMC's hot, luminous stars, using the multi-fibre 2-degree Field (2dF) instrument of the Anglo-Australian Telescope (AAT), primarily in order to investigate directly the massive-star IMF.

sources.summary: The paper uses SMC A-type supergiant abundances to test metallicity-dependent mixing, finding stronger nitrogen enrichment at 0.2 solar metallicity while noting the SMC sample is more massive.

quotes.quote: A comparison of Galactic and SMC A-type supergiant abundances (Venn 1999; Venn Przybilla 2003) suggests that most of the objects have undergone substantial mixing with CN-cycled material, see Fig. mixing . The efficiency of rotational mixing appears to correlate with metallicity since the SMC stars (at 0.2 solar metallicity) show larger nitrogen enrichments.
Note however, that the sample objects in the SMC are on average more massive than the Galactic supergiants.

sources.summary: The paper cites the Small Magellanic Cloud star formation history as showing bursts near Milky Way pericenters, with tidal triggering contributing 10-70% and substantial gas infall needed for a good fit.

quotes.quote: The star formation history in the Small Magellanic Cloud reveals some bursts corresponding to pericenters with the Milky Way (Zaritsky Harris 2004). The tidal-induced fraction of star formation could be between 10 and 70 . A good fit is impossible however without large amounts of gas infall, at least 50 .

sources.summary: The paper describes SMC dust as lacking the 2175 bump, having lower dust and metal abundances, and giving a poorer quasar-reddening fit than CCM or FTZ laws.

quotes.quote: In the UV, however, the extinction curve for the Small Magellanic Cloud (SMC) differs significantly: the curve is almost linear in ^ 1 , i.e. the Galactic type dust bump at 2175 is not present. The SMC have lower dust and metal abundances than the Milky Way and may be more representative of high redshift galaxies.
We have also modeled the quasar colours with extinction by SMC type dust but the fit is worse than for the CCM and FTZ parametrisations.

sources.summary: The paper treats the SMC as part of the interacting Magellanic system, arguing that tidal compression of its outer gas forms young inter-Cloud stars with lower metallicity.

quotes.quote: We discuss how tidal interaction between the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC), the Small Magellanic Cloud (SMC), and the Galaxy triggers galaxy-wide star formation in the Clouds for the last 0.2 Gyr based on our chemodynamical simulations on the Clouds.

The tidal interaction can dramatically compress gas initially within the outer part of the SMC so that new stars can be formed from the gas to become intergalactic young stars in the inter-Cloud region (e.g., the Magellanic Bridge). The metallicity distribution function of the newly formed stars in the Magellanic Bridge has a peak of [ Fe/H ] 0.8 , which is significantly lower than the stellar metallicity of the SMC.
